About Research Open Source Personal Contact

Building tools, experimenting with ideas, and slowly bridging software engineering with astrophysics.

Open Source

In some of my spare time, I’ve been revisiting a lot of the core material from undergraduate physics and astrophysics. Part of that process has been trying to bridge the gap between my current work in software engineering and the research areas I’d eventually like to move further into.

One of the ways I’ve approached that is through open source contributions in astrophysics software. I ended up choosing the galactic dynamics library galpy, partly because I used it during my undergraduate studies, and partly because the maintainers had already provided a list of interesting potential extensions and research-adjacent problems to work on.

It’s been a genuinely enjoyable way to learn again - reading papers, implementing methods, debugging numerical behaviour, and slowly becoming more comfortable working closer to the underlying physics rather than purely infrastructure and backend systems.

Hopefully this is just the beginning of a much longer list of projects.

Numerical integration methods sit underneath a huge amount of modern computational astrophysics.

IAS15 Numerical Integrator

One of my first major contributions involved implementing the IAS15 numerical integrator. IAS15 is a high-order adaptive integrator designed for extremely accurate long-term orbital integration, particularly in gravitational systems where numerical stability matters over very long timescales.

This project was a great introduction to the practical side of computational astrophysics — translating mathematical descriptions from papers into reliable code, understanding error behaviour, and working within an established scientific codebase.

It also gave me a much deeper appreciation for how much care and engineering sits behind the scientific software that researchers often use every day without thinking twice about it.

Density profiles provide simplified models for understanding galactic structure and dark matter distributions.

Einasto Density Profile

Another project involved implementing the Einasto density profile as a galactic potential. The Einasto profile appears frequently in studies of galactic structure and dark matter halo modelling, and has become an important tool in modern galactic dynamics.

What I enjoyed most here was the balance between physical modelling and software design. Scientific code often needs to be both mathematically rigorous and flexible enough for other researchers to build upon later.

Working through these implementations has been one of the most effective ways I’ve found to properly understand the underlying literature rather than only reading it passively.

Estimating orbital actions efficiently remains an important problem in Galactic dynamics.

(WIP) Triaxial Stäckel Action Estimation

More recently, I’ve started exploring methods for approximating orbital actions, particularly the Triaxial Stäckel approximation method.

This is still very much a work in progress, but it’s become one of the most interesting areas I’ve looked at so far. There’s something satisfying about problems that sit halfway between geometry, physics, and numerical optimisation.

I’m especially interested in how these kinds of quantities might be estimated more efficiently, particularly as modern Galactic surveys continue pushing computational limits. Even just implementing existing methods has already taught me a huge amount about the structure of galactic dynamics problems.

Over time I’d like this page to grow into a collection of projects, notes, implementations, and lessons learned while working closer to computational astrophysics.
